summary.detailed_summary: EvoBrain proposes a task-aware continual learning framework to replace static, task-isolated fine-tuning for EEG foundation models. It introduces Neuro-Spectral Task Normalization (NSN) to align temporal statistics and recalibrate spectral responses, alongside Response-Affinity Distillation (RAD) to preserve old-task response geometry and enable selective knowledge transfer. Evaluations across six diverse BCI tasks using four different foundation backbones demonstrate that the model effectively balances plasticity and stability, retaining over 91% of single-task performance.
key_points: Method for post-training EEG foundation models: EvoBrain enables a single unified backbone to incrementally learn diverse BCI tasks via a continual learning paradigm.
Combines Neuro-Spectral Task Normalization (NSN) for distributional alignment and Response-Affinity Distillation (RAD) to mitigate catastrophic forgetting.
Demonstrates architecture-agnostic efficacy across Transformer and Mamba backbones, maintaining low forgetting rates across six heterogeneous EEG datasets.
limitations: Assumes known task boundaries and task-specific heads.
Relies on replay memory, which may raise privacy concerns in clinical settings.
Lacks subject-level validation for the spectral modulation patterns.
Does not address online settings with ambiguous task identities.

method.architecture: Architecture-agnostic framework integrating Neuro-Spectral Task Normalization and Response-Affinity Distillation.
method.finetuning: Continual learning via task-aware normalization, response-geometry distillation, and time-dependent replay.
method.objective: Supervised classification with distillation loss to preserve previous task knowledge.
method.pretraining: Utilizes existing EEG-FMs (CBraMod, EEGMamba, CSBrain, DeeperBrain) as backbones.

summary.one_liner: A continual learning framework for EEG foundation models that enables a single backbone to evolve across heterogeneous BCI tasks without catastrophic forgetting.
summary.title: EvoBrain: Continual Learning of EEG Foundation Models Across Heterogeneous BCI Tasks
summary.unique_contribution: Pioneers cross-task continual learning for EEG foundation models using a combination of neuro-spectral normalization and affinity-aware response distillation.

papers.title: EvoBrain: Continual Learning of EEG Foundation Models Across Heterogeneous BCI Tasks

reasons: Explicitly targets the post-training and downstream adaptation of EEG foundation models.
Proposes a continual learning framework (EvoBrain) specifically for unified EEG decoding using foundation model backbones.
Fits the ecosystem criteria for adaptation, fine-tuning, and scaling analysis of EEG-FMs.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper introduces FMScope, a frozen-representation diagnostic protocol consisting of five tools to audit EEG foundation models for subject-identity confounding. It reveals a universal 'Identity Trap' where subject variance dominates representations and is amplified during fine-tuning, regardless of the clinical label's presence. Evidence from three FMs across four clinical datasets shows that removing the subject axis—often carried by the aperiodic 1/f background—can improve label decoding when a genuine cross-subject marker exists.

headline_results: Subject-variance fraction is 13-89x a random null
Erasing subject axis improves label decoding by +6 to +12 percentage points in primary cells
tasks: Clinical resting-state EEG classification (mental arithmetic, Alzheimer's/FTD, sleep deprivation, trait stress)

key_points: Benchmark diagnostic framework: FMScope audits frozen EEG-FM representations to distinguish genuine clinical biomarkers from subject-identity shortcuts.
Discovery of the 'Identity Trap': Subject variance dominates representations across tested FMs and is amplified during fine-tuning, often acting as a shortcut.
Physiological carrier: For LaBraM and CBraMod, the aperiodic 1/f background is a primary carrier of subject identity, which can be linearly erased to improve label decoding.
limitations: Relies on one dataset per cell in the 2x2 layout
Small sample sizes (N=14 to 65 subjects per dataset)
Aperiodic ablation is a correlational intervention, not necessarily causal
Single-session ADFTD cannot separate disease state from stable traits by data alone

method.finetuning: Linear classification head, full fine-tuning vs. frozen linear probe

summary.one_liner: A diagnostic audit revealing that EEG foundation models often rely on subject-identity shortcuts (the 'Identity Trap') rather than genuine clinical biomarkers.
summary.title: The Identity Trap in EEG Foundation Models: A Diagnostic Audit
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces FMScope, a diagnostic framework to detect and linearly erase subject-identity confounding in frozen EEG-FM representations.

papers.title: The Identity Trap in EEG Foundation Models: A Diagnostic Audit

reasons: Explicitly targets EEG foundation models (FMs) in both title and abstract.
Proposes FMScope, a diagnostic protocol specifically for auditing frozen representations of EEG-FMs.
Evaluates multiple existing EEG-FMs, including LaBraM, CBraMod, and REVE.
Fits the inclusion criteria for EEG-FM ecosystem papers focusing on evaluation and diagnostic auditing.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes a spectral audit framework that uses phase-preserving Fourier interventions to decompose physiological signals into aperiodic and periodic components. It demonstrates that model reliance on aperiodic structure is task-dependent, being dominant in sleep-wake classification and clinical abnormality detection but minimal in motor imagery. The study reveals that six of seven evaluated EEG foundation models and several ECG architectures rely significantly on these broadband features, which can be confounded by demographics like age.

headline_results: Sleep-wake classification showed high aperiodic reliance (BA drop > 0.42).
6/7 EEG foundation models showed significant aperiodic reliance on clinical EEG.
ECG models showed high aperiodic reliance (BA drop 0.32-0.36).

key_points: Benchmark framework: A spectral audit tool that quantifies model reliance on aperiodic 1/f-like envelopes across EEG and ECG tasks.
Foundation model vulnerability: Six of seven EEG foundation models showed significant aperiodic reliance on clinical EEG, suggesting scale does not eliminate broadband shortcuts.
Task-specific findings: Aperiodic reliance is high for sleep-wake and clinical abnormality detection but minimal for motor imagery.
limitations: ECG SpecParam fit quality was lower than EEG due to QRS harmonics.
PhysioNet MI trials are short, making spectral fitting more challenging.
BENDR showed intervention fragility (sham collapse), making its aperiodic reliance estimate unreliable.
The framework identifies reliance but does not provide a training-time solution to eliminate it.

method.objective: Spectral audit using phase-preserving Fourier interventions (sham, aperiodic-shaped, and flattened) to test sufficiency and necessity of spectral components.

summary.one_liner: Introduces a spectral audit framework to quantify how much EEG and ECG deep learning models rely on aperiodic 1/f-like spectral envelopes versus periodic oscillatory rhythms.
summary.title: A spectral audit framework reveals task-dependent aperiodic reliance across EEG and ECG deep learning
summary.unique_contribution: A diagnostic framework using phase-preserving Fourier interventions to disentangle aperiodic spectral reliance from oscillatory biomarkers in physiological time-series models.

papers.title: A spectral audit framework reveals task-dependent aperiodic reliance across EEG and ECG deep learning

reasons: Explicitly evaluates 'six of seven EEG foundation models' using a spectral audit framework.
Focuses on the interpretability and aperiodic reliance of representations within EEG-FMs.
Fits the ecosystem criteria for evaluation and benchmarking of foundation models.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes a joint privacy audit framework for EEG foundation models (BIOT, LaBraM, EEGPT) that evaluates raw reconstruction, membership inference, identity linkage, and attribute leakage. It introduces a cross-encoder transfer audit where a ridge attribute decoder from one frozen encoder transfers to others via a linear bridge, proving that spectral attributes persist across different architectures and datasets. Empirical results across EEGMMI, Sleep-EDF, LIMO, and CHB-MIT show that standard defenses, including DP-SGD and noise injection, fail to eliminate attribute leakage without destroying task utility.

headline_results: AEDS positive (p < 0.001) across all subject-disjoint cells
Attribute leakage persists despite DP-SGD and Gaussian noise defenses

key_points: Benchmark audit of EEG foundation models: Shows that BIOT, LaBraM, and EEGPT embeddings leak spectral attributes even when they pass raw-reconstruction and membership inference tests.
Cross-encoder transferability: Demonstrates that attribute decoders trained on one model can be bridged to others, indicating a shared vulnerability across different EEG-FM architectures.
Defense failure: Finds that DP-SGD and Gaussian noise cannot close the attribute leakage channel while maintaining downstream task utility.
limitations: Does not establish population-level cross-dataset generality.
Evidence is limited to specific datasets and lacks larger clinical corpora like TUH.
Training-noise grid is a proxy and lacks a formal DP accountant.
The event-level task head used in some probes is relatively weak.
Subject-disjoint gains show transport within finite splits, not a universal law.

method.architecture: Audit framework using ridge, MLP, and kNN decoders to extract PSD attributes; employs a linear bridge for cross-encoder transfer.
method.finetuning: Linear bridge and ridge regression for attribute transfer probes.
method.objective: Audit-Endpoint Disagreement Score (AEDS) to quantify privacy leakage across multiple endpoints.
method.pretraining: Audits existing pretrained models (BIOT, LaBraM, EEGPT).

summary.one_liner: An audit framework demonstrating that EEG foundation model embeddings leak spectral attributes even when standard privacy endpoints like raw reconstruction and membership inference are cleared.
summary.title: Pretrained, Frozen, Still Leaking: Auditing Cross-Encoder Attribute Transfer in EEG Foundation Models
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a cross-encoder transfer audit and the Audit-Endpoint Disagreement Score (AEDS) to reveal spectral attribute leakage that single-endpoint privacy audits typically miss.

papers.title: Pretrained, Frozen, Still Leaking: Auditing Cross-Encoder Attribute Transfer in EEG Foundation Models

reasons: Explicitly audits and evaluates existing EEG foundation models including BIOT, LaBraM, and EEGPT.
Focuses on the security and privacy properties of pretrained EEG representations.
Fits the inclusion criteria for EEG-FM ecosystem papers regarding evaluation and auditing.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes Hypnos, a foundation model trained on over 20,000 overnight polysomnography recordings across eight sensing modalities. Unlike prior work relying on masked reconstruction or contrastive objectives, Hypnos employs an auto-regressive RQ-Transformer to predict the next discrete token. Evaluation across sleep staging and cardiac benchmarks shows it significantly outperforms existing foundation models and matches supervised baselines using only 1% of the labeled data.

headline_results: Matches U-Sleep performance using 1% of labeled data.
Outperforms OSF, SleepFM, and sleep2vec on sleep staging.
Surpasses dedicated ECG foundation models on cardiac benchmarks.

key_points: New EEG foundation model: Hypnos uses an auto-regressive RQ-Transformer to learn representations from multi-modal sleep data via next-token prediction.
Demonstrates high sample efficiency, matching supervised sleep staging performance on held-out sets while using 100x less labeled data.
Shows strong cross-domain generalization, surpassing a dedicated ECG foundation model on atrial fibrillation detection tasks.
limitations: Does not yet generalize to arbitrary high-density EEG electrode configurations.
Context length is limited and does not yet scale to hours or days of data.
Limited evaluation on broad clinical outcomes compared to some prior work.

method.architecture: RQ-Transformer (Temporal and Depth Transformers) with RVQ-based tokenization using SeaNet.
method.finetuning: Linear probing and fine-tuning on downstream sleep and cardiac tasks.
method.objective: Next-token prediction (conditional log-likelihood over next residual tokens).
method.pretraining: Self-supervised training on 20,000+ PSG recordings using CRP-based group masking for modality robustness.

summary.one_liner: Hypnos is a multi-modal sleep foundation model that utilizes next-token prediction on RVQ-tokenized physiological signals to learn generalisable representations.
summary.title: Next-Token Prediction Learns Generalisable Representations of Sleep Physiology
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces next-token prediction as a scalable self-supervised objective for multi-modal physiological signals, unifying generative modeling and representation learning.

papers.title: Next-Token Prediction Learns Generalisable Representations of Sleep Physiology

reasons: Explicitly describes 'Hypnos' as a multi-modal sleep foundation model.
Includes EEG as one of the core sensing modalities used for pretraining.
Focuses on learning generalisable representations for various downstream tasks.
Compares performance against other existing foundation models.
